description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a graph (e.g., where the function is increasing or decreasing, linear or nonlinear). Sketch or determine a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a function that has been described verbally.
description_plain: Your child describes how two quantities relate by looking at graphs and can draw a graph based on a description.

subject: Math
description: Construct a function to model a linear relationship between two quantities. Determine the rate of change and initial value of the function from a description of a relationship or from two (x, y) values, including reading these from a table or from a graph. Interpret the rate of change and initial value of a linear function in terms of the situation it models and in terms of its graph or a table of values.
description_plain: Your child can create a function that shows a straight-line relationship between two things, find how fast one changes compared to the other, and understand what the starting value means using tables, graphs, or descriptions.

subject: Math
description: Represent or interpret functional relationships between quantities using tables, graphs, and descriptions.
description_plain: Your child can show and understand how two quantities relate using tables, graphs, or words.

subject: Math
description: Use functions to model relationships between quantities.
description_plain: Your child uses functions to explain how two quantities are connected.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the equation y = mx + b as defining a linear function whose graph is a straight line; give examples of functions that are not linear.
description_plain: Your child understands that the equation y = mx + b makes a straight line and can give examples of functions that don’t make straight lines.

subject: Math
description: Compare properties of two functions, each represented in a different way (i.e., algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions). Example: Given a linear function represented by a table of values and a linear function represented by an algebraic expression, determine which function has the greater rate of change.
description_plain: Your child can compare two functions shown in different ways, like a table and an equation, to decide which one changes faster.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a relation is a function.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a set of pairs of numbers is a function, meaning each input has only one output.

subject: Math
description: Define, evaluate, and compare functions displayed algebraically, graphically, or numerically in tables or by verbal descriptions.
description_plain: Your child can define, find values for, and compare functions shown as equations, graphs, tables, or words.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and interpret functions.
description_plain: Your child can study and explain functions.

subject: Math
description: Functions
description_plain: Functions

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems leading to two linear equations in two variables. Example: Given coordinates for two pairs of points, determine whether the line through the first pair of points intersects the line through the second pair.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems with two linear equations and find out if two lines cross by using points.

subject: Math
description: Solve systems of two linear equations in two variables algebraically and estimate solutions by graphing the equations. Solve simple cases by inspection. Example: 3x + 2y = 5 and 3x + 2y = 6 have no solution because 3x + 2y cannot simultaneously be 5 and 6.
description_plain: Your child can solve two linear equations using algebra, estimate answers by graphing, and recognize when no solution exists.

subject: Math
description: Interpret solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables as points of intersection of their graphs because points of intersection satisfy both equations simultaneously.
description_plain: Your child understands that the solution to two linear equations is where their graphs cross, because that point fits both equations.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations that have rational number coefficients, including equations whose solutions require expanding expressions using the distributive property and collecting like terms.
description_plain: Your child can solve linear equations with fractions or decimals by expanding and combining like terms.

subject: Math
description: Write and identify linear equations in one variable with one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions. Show which of these possibilities is the case by successively transforming the given equation into simpler forms until an equivalent equation of the form x = a, a = a, or a = b results (where a and b are different numbers).
description_plain: Your child can write and recognize linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solutions by simplifying them step-by-step.

subject: Math
description: Write, solve, graph, and interpret linear equations in one or two variables, using various methods.
description_plain: Your child can write, solve, graph, and explain linear equations with one or two variables using different methods.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve linear equations and pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
description_plain: Your child can analyze and solve single linear equations and pairs of linear equations.

subject: Math
description: Derive the equation y = mx for a line through the origin and the equation y = mx + b for a line intercepting the vertical axis at b.
description_plain: Your child can write the equation for a line through the origin as y = mx and for a line crossing the vertical axis at b as y = mx + b.

subject: Math
description: Use similar right triangles to show and explain why the slope m is the same between any two distinct points on a non-vertical line in the coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can use similar triangles to explain why the slope is the same between any two points on a straight, non-vertical line.

subject: Math
description: Graph proportional relationships, interpreting the unit rate as the slope of the graph. Compare two different proportional relationships represented in different ways. Example: Compare a distance-time graph to a distance-time equation to determine which of two moving objects has greater speed.
description_plain: Your child can graph proportional relationships, understand the unit rate as the slope, and compare two different proportional relationships to see which is faster.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and describe linear relationships between two variables, using slope.
description_plain: Your child can study and describe straight-line relationships between two variables by using slope.

subject: Math
description: Understand the connections between proportional relationships, lines, and linear equations.
description_plain: Your child understands how proportional relationships, lines, and linear equations are connected.

subject: Math
description: Perform operations with numbers expressed in scientific notation, including problems where both decimal and scientific notation are used. Express answers in scientific notation and choose units of appropriate size for measurements of very large or very small quantities (e.g., use millimeters per year for seafloor spreading). Interpret scientific notation that has been generated by technology (e.g., interpret 4.7EE9 displayed on a calculator as 4.7 × 109 ).
description_plain: Your child can do math with very big or very small numbers written in scientific notation, choose good units for measurements, and understand scientific notation shown by calculators.

subject: Math
description: Estimate very large or very small quantities by using numbers expressed in the form of a single digit times an integer power of 10 and express how many times larger or smaller one number is than another. Example: Estimate the population of the United States as 3 × 108 and the population of the world as 7 × 109 and determine that the world population is more than 20 times larger than the United States’ population.
description_plain: Your child can estimate very large or small numbers using powers of 10 and compare how many times bigger or smaller one number is than another.

subject: Math
description: Use square root and cube root symbols to represent solutions to equations of the form x2 = p and x3 = p, where p is a positive rational number. Evaluate square roots of perfect squares (up to and including 122 ) and cube roots of perfect cubes (up to and including 53 ) without a calculator. Example: If x2 = 25 then x = ±√25.
description_plain: Your child can use square root and cube root symbols to solve equations and find roots of perfect squares and cubes without a calculator.

subject: Math
description: Apply one or more properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions without a calculator (with final answers expressed in exponential form with positive exponents). Properties will be provided. Example: 312 × 3 ⎯15 = 3 ⎯3 = 1/(33 )
description_plain: Your child can use rules of exponents to rewrite expressions without a calculator, showing answers with positive exponents.

subject: Math
description: Represent and use expressions and equations to solve problems involving radicals and integer exponents.
description_plain: Your child can use expressions and equations with roots and exponents to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate an understanding of expressions and equations with radicals and integer exponents.
description_plain: Your child understands expressions and equations that include roots and whole number exponents.

description_plain: Your child can use close rational numbers to compare and put irrational numbers in order.

description_plain: Your child can estimate the value of irrational numbers without a calculator, like knowing √5 is a bit more than 2.

subject: Math
description: Convert a terminating or repeating decimal to a rational number (limit repeating decimals to thousandths).

description: Demonstrate an understanding of rational and irrational numbers
description_plain: Your child understands the difference between rational and irrational numbers.

subject: Math
description: M03.A-F.1.1.1: Demonstrate that when a whole or set is partitioned into y equal parts, the fraction 1/y represents 1 part of the whole and/or the fraction x/y represents x equal parts of the whole (limit denominators to 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8; limit numerators to whole numbers less than the denominator; and no simplification necessary).
description_plain: Your child understands that when a whole is split into equal parts, a fraction shows one or more of those parts.

subject: Math
description: M03.A-F.1.1 Explore and develop an understanding of fractions as numbers.
description_plain: Your child is learning about fractions and how they work as numbers.

subject: Math
description: M03.A-F.1 Algebraic Concepts
description_plain: M03.A-F.1 Algebraic Concepts

subject: Math
description: M03.A-T.1.1.4: Order a set of whole numbers from least to greatest or greatest to least (up through 9,999, and limit sets to no more than four numbers).
description_plain: Your child can put up to four whole numbers (up to 9,999) in order from smallest to largest or largest to smallest.

subject: Math
description: M03.A-T.1.1.3: Multiply one-digit whole numbers by two-digit multiples of 10 (from 10 through 90)
description_plain: Your child can multiply one-digit numbers by two-digit numbers that are multiples of 10 (like 10, 20, up to 90).

subject: Math
description: M03.A-T.1.1.2: Add two- and three-digit whole numbers (limit sums from 100 through 1,000) and/or subtract two- and three-digit numbers from three-digit whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can add two- and three-digit numbers with sums between 100 and 1,000, and subtract two- or three-digit numbers from three-digit numbers.

subject: Math
description: M03.A-T.1.1.1: Round two- and three-digit whole numbers to the nearest ten or hundred, respectively.
description_plain: Your child can round two-digit numbers to the nearest ten and three-digit numbers to the nearest hundred.

subject: Math
description: M03.A-T.1.1 Apply place-value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child understands place value and uses it to do math with numbers that have more than one digit.

subject: Math
description: M03.A-T.1 Numbers and Operations
description_plain: Your child works with numbers and learns how to add, subtract, multiply, and divide them.

subject: Math
description: Estimate the answer to addition, subtraction, and multiplication problems using whole numbers through six digits (for multiplication, no more than 2 digits × 1 digit, excluding powers of 10).
description_plain: Your child can estimate answers to addition, subtraction, and multiplication problems with whole numbers up to six digits.

subject: Math
description: Divide up to four-digit dividends by one-digit divisors with answers written as whole-number quotients and remainders.
description_plain: Your child can divide numbers up to four digits by one-digit numbers and write the answer with whole number quotients and remainders.

subject: Math
description: Multiply a whole number of up to four digits by a one-digit whole number and multiply 2 two-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a whole number up to four digits by a one-digit number and multiply two two-digit numbers.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers (limit sums and subtrahends up to and including 1,000,000).
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract large whole numbers up to 1,000,000.

subject: Math
description: Use operations to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child uses addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division to solve math problems.
